ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'steamed filled bun' class (FOODON_03315671); no specific vegetarian char siew pau entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers steamed buns with filling as a broad category; no vegetarian char siew pau-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for vegetarian char siew pau or steamed filled bun as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard vegetarian char siew pau weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single dim sum piece or light snack.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Char Siew Pau — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Char Siew Pau (cr_id F06010002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this exact record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun with filling — analogous GI estimate based on steamed Chinese bun (baozi) data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian char siew pau. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed Chinese wheat buns (baozi/pau), which report GI values in the range 55–68 depending on filling and flour refinement. The refined wheat dough and moderate sugar content of this pau support a medium GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (39.26 g/100g × 80 g serving = 31.4 g) / 100 = 20.4, rounded to 20.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 419 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one piece per occasion and avoid adding soy sauce or chilli sauce, as the sodium content of 419 mg/100 g already approaches a significant proportion of the 2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (39.3 g/100 g) combined with elevated sugar (13.3 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to one piece maximum per meal, pair with a high-fibre vegetable dish to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the estimated medium-to-high glycaemic load of approximately 20 per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (419 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (5.46 g/100 g) from processed mock meat filling; saturated fat breakdown unavailable but soy-based gluten filling typically contains modest saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit this pau to occasional consumption due to its elevated sodium content and moderate fat from processed gluten filling, and should choose preparations without added oil or high-sodium sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Char Siew Pau (素叉烧包, Su Cha Shao Bao) is a Singaporean Chinese steamed bun filled with a sweet-savoury mock char siew mixture made from vital wheat gluten and black mushrooms, seasoned with soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and vegetarian oyster sauce. The bun is made from soft, leavened wheat flour dough and steamed until fluffy and slightly glossy. It is a vegetarian adaptation of the classic Cantonese char siew pau (BBQ pork bun), commonly found at dim sum restaurants, hawker centres, and Chinese bakeries across Singapore. The texture is soft and pillowy on the outside with a moist, slightly chewy filling that mimics the sweet-savoury profile of traditional char siew. Per 100 g, it provides 250 kcal, 10.94 g protein, 5.46 g fat, 39.26 g carbohydrate, 13.3 g sugar, 2.21 g dietary fibre, and 419 mg sodium. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content combined with modest fibre results in a medium-to-high glycaemic load, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium level of 419 mg/100 g is a concern for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for vegetarians but contains gluten and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegans due to potential egg or dairy traces in some preparations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06010002) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary regional approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item as of 2026-04-16. Calcium (27.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.58 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and included. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed Chinese wheat bun (baozi) data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for vegetarian char siew pau was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving (80 g) calculated as 65 × (39.26 × 0.80) / 100 = 20.5. The is_halal flag is set to false as halal certification status is not confirmed in the HPB source record; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. Vegan flag is false as some preparations may include egg wash or dairy traces.
